ultimateGoal.summary: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
ultimateGoal.detailedIntent:
The finished product should feel like a polished recruiting command center for a junior undergraduate targeting top quant research, quant trading, systematic trading, and ML-adjacent finance roles.
It should combine a large structured question bank, company-specific interview guides, step-by-step answer explanations, interactive practice modes, mini-games, progress tracking, recruiting roadmaps, compensation research, and source-aware interview intelligence into one cohesive localhost website.
Prioritize correctness, explanation depth, source traceability, repeat-practice value, content organization, and long-term maintainability over shallow visual flash, while still making the interface feel premium, modern, fast, and enjoyable to use.
The system should impress a serious quant club member, quant researcher, trader, or technically strong recruiter by making quant recruiting preparation feel unified, rigorous, practical, and addictive enough to practice daily.
successCriteria: The app provides a polished landing dashboard showing today’s recommended practice, weak topics, mastered topics, recent questions, progress streaks, upcoming recruiting focus areas, company targets, and quick links into timed drills, mock interviews, company pages, mini-games, and study plans.

The app includes major content sections for Quant Research, Quant Trading, Probability, Statistics, Expected Value, Brain Teasers, Mental Math, Market Making, Options and Derivatives, Coding, Python/Data Analysis, Machine Learning, Company Guides, Mock Interviews, Mini-Games, Progress, Resources, and Recruiting Roadmap.

The app includes a structured question bank with questions, answers, hints, step-by-step explanations, tags, topic categories, difficulty levels, estimated time, interview round type, role relevance, firm associations, source notes, and mastery status.

The app includes a substantial seed question bank covering probability, conditional probability, Bayes’ rule, expected value, variance, distributions, order statistics, Markov chains, stochastic processes, random walks, combinatorics, game theory, estimation, Fermi problems, statistics, hypothesis testing, regression, linear algebra, optimization, market making, bid/ask spreads, adverse selection, inventory risk, options intuition, Greeks, arbitrage, trading intuition, algorithms, data structures, Python, pandas, NumPy, SQL basics, and machine learning fundamentals.
The app includes Green Book-style probability and brain-teaser questions, but should create original/paraphrased variants and explanations rather than copying copyrighted book text verbatim.

The app includes original practice questions created specifically for the platform, with realistic quant-interview style wording and detailed explanations that teach the reasoning process instead of only giving the final answer.

The app includes curated questions inspired by public interview reports, public forums, public videos, public blogs, quant prep websites, Reddit discussions, YouTube videos, GitHub resources, and other accessible public materials, with source metadata stored for each externally inspired item.

The app includes a content model that distinguishes original questions, paraphrased public-report questions, user-created questions, Green Book-style questions, coding questions, company-reported questions, and compensation/recruiting notes.

The app includes source fields for each externally inspired item, including source title, source type, source URL where available, date accessed, reported firm, reported role, reported round, reliability level, and notes about whether the question is directly reported, paraphrased, generalized, or original.

The app avoids copying large copyrighted passages, paywalled content, private databases, or proprietary prep-course material, and instead stores concise paraphrases, summaries, metadata, and original explanations.
The app includes a powerful search and filtering system across questions, explanations, firms, tags, topics, difficulty, role type, source type, interview round, and mastery status.

The app includes question detail pages with the prompt, answer, multiple hints, full explanation, common mistakes, shortcut methods, related concepts, similar questions, company relevance, and practice history.
The app includes progressive hint reveal functionality so I can attempt a question seriously before seeing the full answer.
The app includes a spaced repetition or review queue that prioritizes questions marked difficult, answered incorrectly, skipped, or not reviewed recently.
The app includes mastery controls for each question, such as new, learning, needs review, difficult, mastered, and favorite.
The app persists local progress between sessions using local storage, IndexedDB, SQLite, or another local-first persistence layer appropriate to the project stack.
The app includes timed quiz mode where I can select topics, companies, difficulty levels, and question counts, then receive a score breakdown and review explanations afterward.
The app includes random drill mode for fast repetition across mixed quant topics.
The app includes company-specific mock interview mode that simulates the style of a selected firm using that firm’s reported question categories, pacing, and role type.
The app includes role-specific practice paths for quant trader, quant researcher, quantitative developer, systematic trading intern, ML researcher, and data/strategy research roles.

The app includes a recruiting roadmap that organizes preparation by week, topic, difficulty, and target firm, with recommended sequences for someone preparing for senior-year internship or full-time recruiting.
The app includes a “daily practice” system that recommends a balanced set of problems across mental math, probability, market making, coding, ML, and weak areas.

The app includes progress analytics showing questions attempted, accuracy, average time, streaks, strongest topics, weakest topics, mastered firms, difficult question types, and readiness by role or company.

The app includes a clean resources page with books, websites, videos, channels, blogs, public question lists, probability references, coding references, ML references, and recruiting advice resources.
The app includes company guide pages accessible from a visually polished company directory with cards, logos or text placeholders, role tags, difficulty estimates, and quick stats.

The app includes individual company pages for major quant firms including Jane Street, Citadel Securities, Citadel, Two Sigma, D. E. Shaw, Optiver, SIG, IMC, Hudson River Trading, Jump Trading, DRW, Akuna Capital, Five Rings, Tower Research, Virtu, Old Mission, Flow Traders, Radix, Headlands, Squarepoint, Point72/Cubist, WorldQuant, AQR, Millennium, Balyasny, Schonfeld, Bridgewater, and other relevant firms if reliable public information is available.

Each company page includes a company overview, business model summary, role types, office locations where relevant, recruiting timeline, interview process, round structure, technical topics, behavioral expectations, common question categories, reported interview experiences, preparation advice, and compensation section.
Each company page includes a dedicated question section showing firm-associated questions, reported question categories, similar original practice questions, and mock interview launch buttons.

Each company page includes a compensation section for internship and full-time roles, with approximate ranges, year/date context, location notes, source notes, and disclaimers that figures are reported estimates rather than guaranteed current compensation.

Each company page includes an “Interview Style” section explaining whether the firm tends to emphasize probability puzzles, mental math, market making games, coding, statistics, ML, resume deep-dives, trading games, behavioral questions, or research discussion.
Each company page includes a “How to Prepare” section with recommended platform drills, topics to review, common mistakes, and a suggested 1-week and 4-week prep plan for that firm.

The app includes a compensation data model that can store role, firm, location, year, base salary, signing bonus, performance bonus, hourly internship pay, housing stipend, source, and confidence level.

The app includes a research-ingestion workflow or documented content pipeline for adding new public interview reports, YouTube notes, Reddit posts, blog summaries, Twitter/X posts where publicly accessible, and quant prep site references into structured local data.
The app includes a mini-game hub with fun, repeatable games that reinforce actual interview skills rather than unrelated gamification.
The app includes a mental math speed game with arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, expected value arithmetic, and quick comparison prompts.

The app includes a market-making game where I quote bid/ask spreads under uncertainty, update beliefs from order flow, manage inventory, and receive feedback on spread width, adverse selection, P&L, and risk.
The app includes a probability calibration game where I estimate probabilities or expected values and receive feedback on calibration error.
The app includes an estimation/Fermi game where I break down ambiguous quantities into assumptions and compare against reference estimates.
The app includes an options intuition game that tests directional intuition about delta, gamma, theta, volatility, moneyness, and payoff diagrams without requiring institutional options data.
The app includes a “spot the flaw” game where I identify incorrect reasoning in probability, statistics, ML, or trading explanations.
constraints: If an existing project stack is already present, preserve the existing stack, conventions, scripts, routing, styling system, and architecture unless there is a clear reason to refactor.

If this is a greenfield project, prefer a local-first React, TypeScript, and Vite-style web app with structured local data files and local persistence, unless the repository already indicates a different preferred framework.
The website must be able to run on localhost without requiring paid APIs, private credentials, cloud deployment, user accounts, OAuth, brokerage connections, or external services at runtime.

Research can be performed by the coding agent during development, but the finished app should store useful structured local content and source notes instead of depending on live web scraping every time it runs.

Do not store API keys, tokens, personal credentials, private cookies, private database dumps, private interview databases, or machine-specific absolute paths in source code, committed files, logs, fixtures, or generated artifacts.
Use only publicly available information, original generated questions, user-provided content, and clearly paraphrased summaries of public interview experiences.
Do not bypass logins, paywalls, robots restrictions, terms of service, or access controls to collect questions or compensation data.
Do not copy entire copyrighted prep books, paid-course content, proprietary interview databases, or long verbatim passages from websites, Reddit posts, videos, or books.
Every externally inspired content item should include source metadata and should be paraphrased or summarized when appropriate.

Treat anonymous interview reports, Reddit posts, Twitter/X posts, forum comments, YouTube anecdotes, and compensation reports as uncertain and label them as reported or approximate rather than definitive.
Compensation data must include context such as year, location, role, source type, and confidence level whenever available.
Preserve a clean separation between content data, source metadata, quiz logic, progress tracking, mini-game mechanics, company guide data, and UI components.

Use typed data models and validation at ingestion boundaries so questions, companies, sources, compensation records, practice sessions, progress records, and mini-game results are consistent and inspectable.
Maintain a schema that makes it easy to add new firms, topics, questions, explanations, sources, tags, and game types without rewriting unrelated UI logic.
Build with mock and seed data first, then expand content through structured additions rather than hardcoding scattered content directly inside components.
Do not make the app dependent on one fragile data source, one live website, or one external API provider.
Keep the app suitable for personal educational use and interview preparation, not for commercial redistribution of copyrighted content.
Question explanations must be mathematically and logically checked where possible, with common mistakes and sanity checks included for harder problems.
The app should make uncertainty visible when a company question, interview experience, or salary figure is based on limited or anecdotal public data.
The UI must remain responsive with a large question bank, many companies, many tags, and stored progress history.
The renderer/UI should not directly access secrets or privileged system resources.
Maintain testability by ensuring core logic can run deterministically from local fixture data without live web access.
nonGoals: Do not deploy the website publicly, configure production hosting, buy a domain, create user accounts, add payments, or package a mobile/desktop app unless a future explicit goal authorizes it.
Do not build a social network, public leaderboard, multiplayer game, chat room, or community submission system as part of this goal.
Do not require integration with OpenAI, Anthropic, Reddit, YouTube, Twitter/X, Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, or any other external API for the finished app to function.
Do not implement a full scraping engine that continuously crawls the web from the user’s machine at runtime.
Do not reproduce proprietary interview databases, paid prep-course materials, copyrighted books, or large verbatim excerpts from public sources.
Do not claim that any reported interview question, company process, or compensation figure is guaranteed current or officially confirmed unless the source is official and current.
Do not over-focus on animations, decorative effects, or unnecessary complexity at the expense of content accuracy, practice usefulness, and maintainability.
Do not build an overly broad finance education platform unrelated to quant interviews; keep the scope centered on quant research, quant trading, coding, ML, and recruiting preparation.
Do not ignore source traceability, data quality, or copyright constraints in pursuit of adding more questions.
Do not hardcode my personal private information, credentials, accounts, or machine paths into the repository.
Do not make the app require internet access after the seeded content has been created.
Do not treat machine learning as a generic data science section only; keep it connected to quant research, trading, forecasting, and interview-style reasoning.
Do not automatically send emails, apply to jobs, message recruiters, or interact with external recruiting platforms.
Do not optimize for production-scale multi-user architecture when a local-first personal prep tool is the actual goal.
ultimateGoal.targetAudience:
The primary user is me, a junior undergraduate preparing for senior-year internship and full-time recruiting in quant research, quant trading, systematic trading, and ML-adjacent finance roles.
Secondary evaluators are quant club peers, technically strong finance students, quant researchers, traders, and interviewers who expect rigorous content, clean design, realistic practice, and honest sourcing.
ultimateGoal.qualityBar:
The final project should feel like a polished, professional quant recruiting preparation terminal: fast, structured, rigorous, source-aware, visually appealing, and genuinely useful for daily practice.
Core functionality must be covered by deterministic tests using realistic fixture content, including question loading, topic filtering, company filtering, search, quiz scoring, progress persistence, spaced repetition ranking, company page rendering, compensation record rendering, and mini-game scoring.
The UI should be visually clean, information-dense but not cluttered, with clear navigation between dashboard, question bank, practice modes, company guides, ML prep, coding prep, mini-games, progress, resources, and recruiting roadmap.
Each question should be traceable back to its explanation, tags, difficulty, source status, related topics, and practice history.
Each company page should be traceable back to its company data, reported questions, source notes, compensation records, and recommended practice paths.
Required validation should include type checking, linting, unit tests for core logic, integration tests or component tests for primary flows, and a manual browser review confirming that the dashboard, question bank, company pages, quiz mode, progress persistence, and mini-games work coherently on localhost.
The content should be deep enough that I can repeatedly practice probability, expected value, market making, coding, ML, and company- [truncated from 1898 characters]

goal_plan.latestProgressNote: Summary: Scope one coding pass to make the mental math speed game checklist-complete with local structured data, validation, UI evidence, and smoke coverage.
goal_plan.message:
Implement one bounded batch: turn the existing mental math mini-game into a directly evidenced, checklist-complete speed game. Focus on src/data/mentalMath.ts, src/lib/mentalMathValidation.ts, src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts, the existing mini-game UI in src/App.tsx or related components, and smoke/mini-games.spec.ts. Ensure the game covers arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, and expected value arithmetic with timed rounds, difficulty/mode metadata, answer checking, explanations or solution notes, and local-first deterministic content. Strengthen validation so missing required categories, malformed prompts/answers, duplicate IDs, absent explanations, or weak metadata fail tests. Update the UI only as needed to expose category/mode selection, timed play, scoring/streak feedback, and post-answer explanations. Avoid reworking company pages, roadmap, broad question-bank architecture, external APIs, scraping, credentials, deployment, or unrelated visual redesign. The task is done when the mental math checklist item can be marked met with direct data, validation, unit test, build, and smoke-test evidence. Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.

If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.

Acceptance criteria:
- Mental math data includes validated drills for arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, and expected value arithmetic.
- Each drill has stable typed metadata: id, category, difficulty, prompt, correct answer or accepted answer rule, explanation/solution note, estimated time, and source/origin context when relevant.
- Validation fails for missing required categories, duplicate IDs, invalid answers, missing explanations, or incomplete metadata, with unit tests covering pass and fail cases.
- The UI exposes a playable timed mental math speed game with category or mixed-mode practice, score/streak feedback, and answer explanations after attempts.
- smoke/mini-games.spec.ts verifies the mental math game renders, starts a timed round, accepts an answer, updates score/state, and shows explanatory feedback.
- No runtime dependency on external APIs, credentials, paid services, or live web data is introduced.

Integrity will verify with:
- Run npm run test with emphasis on src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts and any game-engine tests touched.
- Run npm run build to confirm TypeScript and production build correctness.
- Run npm run smoke -- smoke/mini-games.spec.ts to capture browser-level evidence for the playable game.
- Manually inspect the updated local data and UI path to confirm every required mental math category is visible and playable.

Ultimate Goal: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.

If this implementation needs live external data, build a provider layer instead of hardcoding local-only mock data. Include demo/mock mode for offline development, a live adapter mode that activates only when local credentials are configured, and explicit missing-credential/error/loading states.

For trading-style work, keep provider choice generic unless the user selected a provider. Free/no-card APIs are allowed. Use credential requests and explicit user-visible approval/input flow for API keys or secrets; do not embed or infer secrets.

If a free/no-card API credential is required to validate live behavior, request it through the project credential flow rather than downgrading to mock-only behavior, embedding a secret, or inventing one.

Outcome strategy:
- Ultimate target: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.

- Current blockers: none.
- Quality bar: The final project should feel like a polished, professional quant recruiting preparation terminal: fast, structured, rigorous, source-aware, visually appealing, and genuinely useful for daily practice. Core functionalit…
- Preserve constraints: If an existing project stack is already present, preserve the existing stack, conventions, scripts, routing, styling system, and architecture unless…; If this is a greenfield project, prefer a local-first React, TypeScript, and Vite-style web app with structured local data files and local persistenc…; The website must be able to run on localhost without requiring paid APIs, private credentials, cloud deployment, user accounts, OAuth, brokerage conn…; Research can be performed by the coding agent during development, but the finished app should store useful structured local content and source notes….
- Avoid non-goals: Do not deploy the website publicly, configure production hosting, buy a domain, create user accounts, add payments, or package a mobile/desktop app u…; Do not build a social network, public leaderboard, multiplayer game, chat room, or community submission system as part of this goal.; Do not require integration with OpenAI, Anthropic, Reddit, YouTube, Twitter/X, Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, or any other external API for the finished app….

canonicalFacts: The app provides a polished landing dashboard showing today’s recommended practice, weak topics, mastered topics, recent questions, progress streaks, upcoming recruiting focus areas, company targets, and quick links into timed drills, mock interviews, company pages, mini-games, and study plans.

The app includes major content sections for Quant Research, Quant Trading, Probability, Statistics, Expected Value, Brain Teasers, Mental Math, Market Making, Options and Derivatives, Coding, Python/Data Analysis, Machine Learning, Company Guides, Mock Interviews, Mini-Games, Progress, Resources, and Recruiting Roadmap.

The app includes a structured question bank with questions, answers, hints, step-by-step explanations, tags, topic categories, difficulty levels, estimated time, interview round type, role relevance, firm associations, source notes, and mastery status.
If an existing project stack is already present, preserve the existing stack, conventions, scripts, routing, styling system, and architecture unless there is a clear reason to refactor.

If this is a greenfield project, prefer a local-first React, TypeScript, and Vite-style web app with structured local data files and local persistence, unless the repository already indicates a different preferred framework.
The website must be able to run on localhost without requiring paid APIs, private credentials, cloud deployment, user accounts, OAuth, brokerage connections, or external services at runtime.
Mental math data includes validated drills for arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, and expected value arithmetic.
Each drill has stable typed metadata: id, category, difficulty, prompt, correct answer or accepted answer rule, explanation/solution note, estimated time, and source/origin context when relevant.
Validation fails for missing required categories, duplicate IDs, invalid answers, missing explanations, or incomplete metadata, with unit tests covering pass and fail cases.

scopedGoal.summary: Scope one coding pass to make the mental math speed game checklist-complete with local structured data, valid…
scopedGoal.executionBrief:
Implement one bounded batch: turn the existing mental math mini-game into a directly evidenced, checklist-complete speed game. Focus on src/data/mentalMath.ts, src/lib/mentalMathValidation.ts, src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts, the existing mini-game UI in src/App.tsx or related components, and smoke/mini-games.spec.ts. Ensure the game covers arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, and expected value arithmetic with timed rounds, difficulty/mode metadata, answer checking, explanations or solution notes, and local-first deterministic content. Strengthen validation so missing required categories, malformed prompts/answers, duplicate IDs, absent explanations, or weak metadata fail tests. Update the UI only as needed to expose category/mode selection, timed play, scoring/streak feedback, and post-answer explanations. Avoid reworking company pages, roadmap, broad question-bank architecture, external APIs, scraping, credentials, deployment, or unrelated visual redesign. The task is done when the mental math checklist item can be marked met with direct data, validation, unit test, build, and smoke-test evidence. Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.
Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.
acceptanceCriteria: Mental math data includes validated drills for arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, and expected value arithmetic.
Each drill has stable typed metadata: id, category, difficulty, prompt, correct answer or accepted answer rule, explanation/solution note, estimated time, and source/origin context when relevant.
Validation fails for missing required categories, duplicate IDs, invalid answers, missing explanations, or incomplete metadata, with unit tests covering pass and fail cases.
The UI exposes a playable timed mental math speed game with category or mixed-mode practice, score/streak feedback, and answer explanations after attempts.
smoke/mini-games.spec.ts verifies the mental math game renders, starts a timed round, accepts an answer, updates score/state, and shows explanatory feedback.
No runtime dependency on external APIs, credentials, paid services, or live web data is introduced.
constraints: Preserve the existing React/TypeScript/Vite stack, routing, styling conventions, and local-first architecture.
Keep content, validation logic, game logic, and UI concerns cleanly separated where the current architecture allows.
Do not copy paid prep material, proprietary question banks, or long verbatim public-source text.
Do not spend this pass re-proving company-page or roadmap checklist items that already have direct evidence.
Keep tests deterministic and runnable from local fixtures only.
testStrategy: Run npm run test with emphasis on src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts and any game-engine tests touched.
Run npm run build to confirm TypeScript and production build correctness.
Run npm run smoke -- smoke/mini-games.spec.ts to capture browser-level evidence for the playable game.
Manually inspect the updated local data and UI path to confirm every required mental math category is visible and playable.

overview.summary: quant_interview_prep is a Vite React TypeScript app for local-first quant interview practice games, with timed prompts, answer submission, and persisted mini-game progress in the browser-facing application.
overview.architecture: The app uses a small React entry point in src/main.tsx, a large stateful App component in src/App.tsx, and supporting data, game-engine, progress-storage, and styling subsystems under src/data, src/lib, and src/styles.
overview.whatProjectDoes: It presents quant interview prep mini-games focused on speed, rigor, and daily practice; index.html describes it as “Local-first quant interview practice games for speed, rigor, and daily prep.” Progress appears local because App imports progressStorage helpers such as readMiniGameProgress and recordCompletedAttempt.
overview.howItIsOrganized: Top-level configuration is npm/Vite/TypeScript-oriented, with package.json scripts for dev, build, typecheck, Vitest, and Playwright smoke tests. Runtime code lives in src, smoke tests live in smoke, and generated/dependency-heavy folders such as node_modules, dist, .git, and .agent-workbench are excluded from the portable scan.
overview.importantToKnowFirst: The main implementation surface is src/App.tsx, which wires routes, game definitions, session state, scoring, and progress summaries. No live market-data, brokerage, or external provider boundary is evident from the scan; the practical interface should treat this as offline demo/local mode, with explicit loading/empty/error states only if future provider adapters or local credentials are added.

subsystemSummaries.name: React application shell
subsystemSummaries.summary: src/main.tsx mounts App into #root and imports global styling; index.html supplies the root element and application metadata.

subsystemSummaries.name: Mini-game UI and orchestration
subsystemSummaries.summary: src/App.tsx imports game definitions, prompt lookup, game session operations, and progress storage, making it the central coordinator for the practice flow.

subsystemSummaries.name: Local data and game logic
subsystemSummaries.summary: src/data appears to hold game definitions and prompts, while src/lib appears to hold reusable game types, engine operations, and progress persistence helpers used by App.

subsystemSummaries.name: Testing and verification
subsystemSummaries.summary: The project has Vitest scripts for unit-style tests and Playwright smoke coverage under smoke, with package.json exposing test:smoke and smoke aliases.

dependencyHighlights: Runtime dependencies are limited to react and react-dom from package.json.
Build and development tooling uses Vite, TypeScript, @vitejs/plugin-react, and React/Node type packages.
Testing dependencies include vitest for test runs and @playwright/test for smoke/browser tests.
The npm lockfile is present as package-lock.json and records the same project version 0.1.0.
overview.statisticsSummary: The scan includes 22 files across 6 folders, totaling 150,036 bytes; TypeScript is the largest included language at 88,361 bytes, followed by JSON at 50,598 bytes and CSS at 10,516 bytes. Tests are present, primary package management is npm, and 2,001 files were excluded, mostly node_modules.
recommendations: Start review in src/App.tsx because it contains the exported App symbol and imports the game engine, game data, and progress storage boundaries.
Use package.json scripts as the operational interface: npm run dev, npm run build, npm run typecheck, npm run test, and npm run smoke.
Keep provider assumptions out of the interface: this project appears local-first and should not require paid APIs, brokerage accounts, billing setup, or portable secrets.

summaryCache.summary: The package is private, versioned 0.1.0, and uses ESM. Scripts cover Vite dev/preview, TypeScript build/typecheck, Vitest tests, and Playwright smoke tests.

summaryCache.title: Playwright smoke test directory
summaryCache.purpose: Contains browser-level smoke coverage for the mini-game interface.
summaryCache.summary: The directory contains smoke/mini-games.spec.ts and is exercised by package.json scripts test:smoke and smoke via Playwright.

summaryCache.title: Application source tree
summaryCache.purpose: Contains the React app, local game data, reusable logic, styles, and Vite environment typing.
summaryCache.summary: The directory contains 13 files in the scan and is organized into src/data, src/lib, src/styles, plus App.tsx, main.tsx, and vite-env.d.ts.

summaryCache.title: Main mini-game application component
summaryCache.purpose: Coordinates routing, game sessions, prompt rendering, answers, timing, and local progress display.
summaryCache.summary: This is the largest visible implementation file and exports App. It defines helpers such as useHashRoute, formatPercent, formatTime, describeAttempt, getDeckSnapshot, and getProgressRollup, and imports gameDefinitions, gameEngine operations, and progressStorage helpers.

summaryCache.title: Game data subsystem
summaryCache.purpose: Holds game definitions and prompt data consumed by the application.
summaryCache.summary: App imports gameDefinitions, getGameDefinition, isGameId, and promptsByGame from this area, so it appears to be the local content source for the mini-games.

summaryCache.title: Game engine and persistence library
summaryCache.purpose: Holds reusable types, session operations, scoring/progress helpers, and local progress storage used by App.
summaryCache.summary: App imports GameDefinition/GamePrompt-related types, session functions such as startGameSession and submitAnswer, and persistence helpers such as readMiniGameProgress and recordCompletedAttempt from this subsystem.

summaryCache.title: React render entry point
summaryCache.purpose: Bootstraps the React application into the HTML root element.
summaryCache.summary: It imports React, ReactDOM, App, and src/styles/global.css, then renders App inside React.StrictMode using ReactDOM.createRoot.

summaryCache.title: Global styling subsystem
summaryCache.purpose: Contains CSS used by the React entry point.
summaryCache.summary: src/main.tsx imports src/styles/global.css, and the scan reports one CSS file totaling 10,516 bytes, making this the app-wide styling location.

Changed files:

 - Tightened local provenance notes for coverage entries so the deck reads as explicitly local-first.
- `src/lib/gameTypes.ts`
 - Added a typed `NumericAnswerUnit` union for stable numeric metadata.
- `src/lib/gameEngine.ts`

- `src/lib/mentalMathValidation.ts`

 - minimum text-length checks for prompts, solutions, explanations, skill targets, and coverage notes

 - duplicate tag detection
 - allowed answer-unit enforcement
 - integer/probability/percent semantics
 - tolerance upper bounds
 - local provenance validation for source notes
- `src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts`
 - Added pass/fail coverage for:
 - stronger metadata rules
 - weak coverage provenance
 - duplicate tags
 - oversized tolerance
 - fraction-form answer acceptance
- `smoke/mini-games.spec.ts`
 - Extended the smoke test to verify post-answer explanatory feedback is actually rendered.

- `npm run test -- src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts src/lib/gameEngine.test.ts` ✅

agents.name: Coding Pass 1
agents.taskPrompt:
Make the largest coherent, reviewable change that materially advances the scoped outcome, preserve existing conventions, prioritize correctness, run relevant checks, and explain changed files and verification results.
Scoped goal from the goal agent: Scope one coding pass to make the mental math speed game checklist-complete with local structured data, valid…
Implement one bounded batch: turn the existing mental math mini-game into a directly evidenced, checklist-complete speed game. Focus on src/data/mentalMath.ts, src/lib/mentalMathValidation.ts, src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts, the existing mini-game UI in src/App.tsx or related components, and smoke/mini-games.spec.ts. Ensure the game covers arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, and expected value arithmetic with timed rounds, difficulty/mode metadata, answer checking, explanations or solution notes, and local-first deterministic content. Strengthen validation so missing required categories, malformed prompts/answers, duplicate IDs, absent explanations, or weak metadata fail tests. Update the UI only as needed to expose category/mode selection, timed play, scoring/streak feedback, and post-answer explanations. Avoid reworking company pages, roadmap, broad question-bank architecture, external APIs, scraping, credentials, deployment, or unrelated visual redesign. The task is done when the mental math checklist item can be marked met with direct data, validation, unit test, build, and smoke-test evidence. Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.
Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.
Acceptance criteria:
- Mental math data includes validated drills for arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, and expected value arithmetic.
- Each drill has stable typed metadata: id, category, difficulty, prompt, correct answer or accepted answer rule, explanation/solution note, estimated time, and source/origin context when relevant.
- Validation fails for missing required categories, duplicate IDs, invalid answers, missing explanations, or incomplete metadata, with unit tests covering pass and fail cases.
- The UI exposes a playable timed mental math speed game with category or mixed-mode practice, score/streak feedback, and answer explanations after attempts.
- smoke/mini-games.spec.ts verifies the mental math game renders, starts a timed round, accepts an answer, updates score/state, and shows explanatory feedback.
- No runtime dependency on external APIs, credentials, paid services, or live web data is introduced.
Integrity will verify with:
- Run npm run test with emphasis on src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts and any game-engine tests touched.
- Run npm run build to confirm TypeScript and production build correctness.
- Run npm run smoke -- smoke/mini-games.spec.ts to capture browser-level evidence for the playable game.
- Manually inspect the updated local data and UI path to confirm every required mental math category is visible and playable.
Ultimate Goal: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.

Inspect the active project and recommend the next bounded tasks for the workflow.
First decide whether the Ultimate Goal is already satisfied strongly enough that the workflow should stop opening new cycles. If it is, mark the goal as satisfied and return zero recommendations.
Return 0 to 5 recommendations.

Plan like a small SWE team. Checklist items are acceptance checks; a cycle is the next coherent task group based on shared paths, tests, evidence, user value, and blocking order.

If required checks are unmet/unknown, rank the next coherent required-check group ahead of generic stabilization unless a real blocker exists. Do not claim met without repository evidence or validation output.

Checklist governance: return goalCheckUpdates only for changed items. Add required checks only when indispensable, consolidate duplicates, mark redundant/non-goal/user-derived removals as not_applicable, and keep evidence concise.
Completion: percentComplete is met required checks divided by total required checks. goal_satisfied requires every required check met and no open blocker.
If the Ultimate Goal is satisfied, set the goal-completion assessment to goal_satisfied and leave the recommendations array empty.
Use only relative project paths in relatedPaths.

agents.name: Coding Pass 1
agents.taskPrompt:
Make the largest coherent, reviewable change that materially advances the scoped outcome, preserve existing conventions, prioritize correctness, run relevant checks, and explain changed files and verification results.
Scoped goal from the goal agent: Scope the next pass to close the highest-impact company-guide evidence gap, not to re-prove already evidenced…
Implement one coherent company-page content/evidence pass. Inspect the existing company data model, validation tests, routing, and company-page UI. For each major quant firm already represented in the app, ensure the local structured data and rendered page include: company overview/business model, role types and relevant locations, recruiting timeline/interview process, a How to Prepare section with drills/topics/mistakes/1-week plan, compensation context for internship and full-time roles with year/location/source-type/uncertainty notes, and a dedicated firm-question section that links or displays firm-associated questions plus similar original practice. Preserve the existing stack, styling, local-first data approach, and source-aware conventions. Do not add external runtime APIs, credentials, scraping, copied proprietary content, or a broad redesign. Update/extend validation so missing company-guide subsections fail deterministically, and add smoke coverage proving several representative company pages render the required sections. Done means the company-page checklist batch has direct repository evidence through typed data, UI rendering, validation tests, and smoke tests. Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.
Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.
Acceptance criteria:
- Major company pages render Overview, Interview Process/Timeline, How to Prepare, Compensation, and Firm Questions sections from local structured data.
- Company data includes source-aware compensation context with role, approximate range, year/date context, location notes, source type, and uncertainty caveat where appropriate.
- How to Prepare content includes recommended platform drills, topics to review, common mistakes, and a suggested 1-week plan for each covered firm.
- Firm-question sections show firm-associated question categories plus similar original practice questions or links into the question bank.
- Validation tests fail on missing required company-guide fields and pass for the completed local dataset.
- Smoke tests cover representative company pages and assert the new sections are visible without network access.
Integrity will verify with:
- Run the relevant company validation unit tests, especially tests covering required sections and source metadata.
- Run the company-page smoke test and add assertions for Overview, How to Prepare, Compensation, and Firm Questions.
- Run question-bank smoke/tests only if firm-question linking or question metadata is changed.
- Run the project build or standard test script used by the repo to confirm the local app still compiles.
Ultimate Goal: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
If this implementation needs live external data, build a provider layer instead of hardcodin [truncated from 13177 characters]

{"type":"agentMessage","id":"msg_01946d035a87f87d016a036713e3c48191a1720720565df204","text":"Implemented the company-page evidence pass focused on the remaining Firm Questions gap.

Changed:
- `src/App.tsx`
 - Added explicit `Firm Questions` section.
 - Renders company-guide original practice seeds from local structured data.
 - Links each seed to its deterministic shared Question Bank record.
- `src/data/questionBank.ts`
 - Added `getCompanyQuestionBankId()` helper so company practice seeds and question-bank routes share one ID convention.
- `src/lib/companyValidation.ts`
 - Added deterministic failure for duplicate company original-practice question IDs.
- `src/lib/questionBankValidation.ts`
 - Now fails if any represented firm lacks at least 2 firm-associated questions or lacks generated company-guide practice coverage.
- Tests updated:
 - `src/lib/companyValidation.test.ts`
 - `src/lib/questionBankValidation.test.ts`
 - `smoke/company-pages.spec.ts`
 - `smoke/question-bank.spec.ts`
- `src/styles/global.css`

agents.name: Goal Agent
agents.taskPrompt:
Turn approved recommendations into scoped, testable execution briefs. Keep plans concrete, bounded, aligned with the outcome strategy, and focused on evidence that moves the Ultimate Goal checklist forward.
Turn the approved recommendation into a scoped goal for the next coding pass.
The output must represent one bounded task for a single cycle, not a broad multi-phase project.
The result must be executable by one coding agent in one pass. If related checks share implementation paths, tests, or evidence, plan the largest coherent reviewable batch instead of narrowing to a tiny evidence-only slice.
Do not scope another coding pass just to re-prove a semantically identical checklist batch that already has direct evidence. If the approved recommendation is about checklist cleanup, focus the brief on the concrete missing evidence or redundant checks named in the recommendation.
`executionBrief` should be a full prompt for the coding agent: what to change, where to focus, what to avoid, and how to know the task is done.
Make acceptanceCriteria concrete and testable.
Keep acceptanceCriteria to at most 6 bullets and testStrategy to at most 4 focused checks.
Use the Goal checklist as the completion source of truth. Prefer a scoped plan that turns a coherent group of unmet or unknown required checks into met checks with evidence.
Keep constraints aligned with the Ultimate Goal and the repository boundaries.
Use the outcome strategy below to keep the scoped plan pointed at the best finished project outcome.
Outcome strategy:
- Ultimate target: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
- Completion source of truth: 55/127 required goal checks met (43%).
- Primary next move: Satisfy the highest-impact coherent batch of related goal checks with direct repository evidence.
- Highest-impact open checks: Each company page includes a “How to Prepare” section with recommended platform drills, topics to review, common mistakes, and a suggested 1-week and…; Each company page includes a company overview, business model summary, role types, office locations where relevant, recruiting timeline, interview pr…; Each company page includes a compensation section for internship and full-time roles, with approximate ranges, year/date context, location notes, sou…; Each company page includes a dedicated question section showing firm-associated questions, reported question categories, similar original practice qu….
- Efficient batching opportunity: includes: The app includes a mental math speed game with arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, expected value arithm…; The app includes a recruiting roadmap that organizes preparation by week, topic, difficulty, and target firm, with recommended sequences for someone…; The app includes individual company pages for major quant firms including Jane Street, Citadel Securities, Citadel, Two Sigma, D. E. Shaw, Optiver, S…; The app includes original practice questions created specifically for the platform, with realistic quant-interview style wording and detailed explana….
- Current blockers: none.
- Quality bar: The final project should feel like a polished, professional quant recruiting preparation terminal: fast, structured, rigorous, source-aware, visually appealing, and genuinely useful for daily practice. Core functionalit…
- Preserve constraints: If an existing project stack is already present, preserve the existing stack, conventions, scripts, routing, styling system, and architecture unless…; If this is a greenfield project, prefer a local-first React, TypeScript, and Vite-style web app with structured local data files and local persistenc…; The website must be able t [truncated from 16198 characters]
agents.lastMessageSnippet: Summary: Scope the next pass to close the highest-impact company-guide evidence gap, not to re-prove already evidenced mental-math or roadmap checks.

events.detail: Summary: Scope the next pass to close the highest-impact company-guide evidence gap, not to re-prove already evidenced mental-math or roadmap checks.
events.raw: {"type":"agentMessage","id":"msg_05b002dfdc11ae47016a03652588d08191a78ab1f7fe0b595f","text":"{\"summary\":\"Scope the next pass to close the highest-impact company-guide evidence gap, not to re-prove already evidenced mental-math or roadmap checks.\",\"executionBrief\":\"Implement one coherent company-page content/evidence pass. Inspect the existing company data model, validation tests, routing, and company-page UI. For each major quant firm already represented in the app, ensure the local structured data and rendered page include: company overview/business model, role types and relevant locations, recruiting timeline/interview process, a How to Prepare section with drills/topics/mistakes/1-week plan, compensation context for internship and full-time roles with year/location/source-type/uncertainty notes, and a dedicated firm-question section that links or displays firm-associated questions plus similar original practice. Preserve the existing stack, styling, local-first data approach, and source-aware conventions. Do not add external runtime APIs, credentials, scraping, copied proprietary content, or a broad redesign. Update/extend validation so missing company-guide subsections fail deterministically, and add smoke coverage proving several representative company pages render the required sections. Done means the company-page checklist batch has direct repository evidence through typed data, UI rendering, validation tests, and smoke tests.\", [truncated from 2180 characters]

agents.taskPrompt:
Make the largest coherent, reviewable change that materially advances the scoped outcome, preserve existing conventions, prioritize correctness, run relevant checks, and explain changed files and verification results.
Scoped goal from the goal agent: Complete the mental-math speed-game evidence batch without duplicating already-proven checklist work.
Implement one focused pass that turns the mental math speed-game checklist batch into direct repository evidence. Start by inspecting the existing mental math data, validation, UI route/component, and tests: src/data/mentalMath.ts, src/lib/mentalMathValidation.ts, src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts, smoke/mini-games.spec.ts, and any current app route wiring. Do not rebuild or re-prove functionality that already has direct passing evidence; instead, close only concrete gaps in required category coverage, validation, or UI/smoke visibility. Ensure the speed game visibly supports arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, and expected-value arithmetic with local structured drills, deterministic validation, timed/scored play, immediate answer feedback, and source/content notes where relevant. Keep changes narrow to the existing stack and conventions. The task is done when the local data model, validation tests, and smoke coverage prove the mental-math speed game is complete enough for the Goal checklist item and no external runtime service is required. Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.
Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.
Acceptance criteria:
- Mental math data includes nontrivial local drill coverage for arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, and expected-value arithmetic.
- Validation enforces required fields, category coverage, unique IDs, answer/explanation presence, difficulty/estimated-time metadata, and deterministic local-only content.
- The app UI exposes the mental math speed game with category selection or mixed mode, timer/scoring/streak behavior, and immediate correctness feedback.
- Smoke coverage exercises loading the mini-games area, starting a mental math session, answering at least one prompt, and observing score/feedback update.
- Existing evidenced checks are not duplicated with redundant tests or parallel data models; any added evidence targets a named gap.
- No paid APIs, credentials, scraping, accounts, or external runtime dependencies are introduced.
Integrity will verify with:
- Run the targeted mental math validation unit tests, especially src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts.
- Run the mini-games smoke test covering the mental math flow: smoke/mini-games.spec.ts.
- Run the broader relevant test/build command used by the repo, such as npm run test and npm run build if available.
- Manually inspect the local UI path to confirm the speed-game experience is polished and checklist-visible.
Ultimate Goal: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
If this implementation needs live external data, build a provider layer instead of hardcoding local-only mock data. Include demo/mock mode for offline dev [truncated from 13059 characters]

events.detail: Implemented one focused mental-math speed-game evidence pass. Changed files: - `src/lib/mentalMathValidation.ts` - Strengthened validation for local-only source metadata: source type, content status, source note, and local content date. - Enforced category prompt minimums for all configured mental-math categories. - Added required difficulty diversity for required calculation families. - `src/lib/mentalMathValidation.test.ts` - Added evidence for duplicate IDs, non-local source metadata rejection, invalid dates/statuses, and shallow single-difficulty decks. - `src/App.tsx` - Added visible Mental Math round mode selector: mixed local categories or focused category. - Focused category rounds now use filtered local prompts, deterministic category-aware seeds, round caps, timer/scoring/streaks, and immediate feedback. - Added category content notes in the coverage panel. - `src/styles/global.css` - Styled the new round-mode panel and improved mental-math coverage cards. - `smoke/mini-games.spec.ts` - Smoke now selects an arithmetic-focused mental-math round, submits a known correct answer, and verifies feedback, score, and streak updates. Verification: [truncated from 1502 characters]

agents.name: Goal Agent
agents.taskPrompt:
Turn approved recommendations into scoped, testable execution briefs. Keep plans concrete, bounded, aligned with the outcome strategy, and focused on evidence that moves the Ultimate Goal checklist forward.
Turn the approved recommendation into a scoped goal for the next coding pass.
The output must represent one bounded task for a single cycle, not a broad multi-phase project.
The result must be executable by one coding agent in one pass. If related checks share implementation paths, tests, or evidence, plan the largest coherent reviewable batch instead of narrowing to a tiny evidence-only slice.
Do not scope another coding pass just to re-prove a semantically identical checklist batch that already has direct evidence. If the approved recommendation is about checklist cleanup, focus the brief on the concrete missing evidence or redundant checks named in the recommendation.
`executionBrief` should be a full prompt for the coding agent: what to change, where to focus, what to avoid, and how to know the task is done.
Make acceptanceCriteria concrete and testable.
Keep acceptanceCriteria to at most 6 bullets and testStrategy to at most 4 focused checks.
Use the Goal checklist as the completion source of truth. Prefer a scoped plan that turns a coherent group of unmet or unknown required checks into met checks with evidence.
Keep constraints aligned with the Ultimate Goal and the repository boundaries.
Use the outcome strategy below to keep the scoped plan pointed at the best finished project outcome.

agents.name: Recommendation Agent
agents.taskPrompt:
Produce ranked next actions based on the repository state, the current cycle, unresolved issues, and the highest-impact unmet Ultimate Goal checks. Perform checklist governance first: add only necessary required checks, remove redundant or no-longer-applicable checks with evidence, and prefer coherent one-cycle batches that improve the finished project outcome with direct evidence.
Inspect the active project and recommend the next bounded tasks for the workflow.
First decide whether the Ultimate Goal is already satisfied strongly enough that the workflow should stop opening new cycles. If it is, mark the goal as satisfied and return zero recommendations.
Return 0 to 5 recommendations.
Output terse JSON only. No greetings, markdown, filler, or restating unchanged logs/checklists.
Plan like a small SWE team. Checklist items are acceptance checks; a cycle is the next coherent task group based on shared paths, tests, evidence, user value, and blocking order.
Recommendations must be concrete single-cycle tasks. Use medium scope for cohesive batches, small only for isolated checks, and split unrelated or umbrella work.
If required checks are unmet/unknown, rank the next coherent required-check group ahead of generic stabilization unless a real blocker exists. Do not claim met without repository evidence or validation output.
For live-data, trading, brokerage, market-data, analytics, or finance interfaces, prefer a provider abstraction with offline demo/mock mode, credentialed live adapter mode, and loading/error/empty states. Paid credentials require operator choice.
Checklist governance: return goalCheckUpdates only for changed items. Add required checks only when indispensable, consolidate duplicates, mark redundant/non-goal/user-derived removals as not_applicable, and keep evidence concise.
Completion: percentComplete is met required checks divided by total required checks. goal_satisfied requires every required check met and no open blocker.
If the Ultimate Goal is satisfied, set the goal-completion assessment to goal_satisfied and leave the recommendations array empty.
Use only relative project paths in relatedPaths.
Use the deterministic outcome strategy below as the decision frame. It is guidance, not a license to ignore concrete repository evidence.
Outcome strategy:
- Ultimate target: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
- Completion source of truth: 50/122 required goal checks met (41%).
- Primary next move: Satisfy the highest-impact coherent batch of related goal checks with direct repository evidence.
- Highest-impact open checks: Each company page includes a “How to Prepare” section with recommended platform drills, topics to review, common mistakes, and a suggested 1-week and…; Each company page includes a company overview, business model summary, role types, office locations where relevant, recruiting timeline, interview pr…; Each company page includes a compensation section for internship and full-time roles, with approximate ranges, year/date context, location notes, sou…; Each company page includes a dedicated question section showing firm-associated questions, reported question categories, similar original practice qu…; Each company page includes an “Interview Style” section explaining whether the firm tends to emphasize probability puzzles, mental math, market makin….
- Efficient batching opportunity: includes: The app includes a mental math speed game with arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, expected value arithm…; The app includes a recruiting roadmap that organizes preparation by week, topic, difficulty, and target firm, with recommended sequences for someone… [truncated from 21144 characters]

Summary: Ultimate Goal is not satisfied; strong company-guide evidence exists, but many required product/content checks remain open.
Goal progress: 45% - 55/122 required checks after 5 evidence-backed updates. `npm run test` passed 62 tests; typecheck was blocked by read-only tsbuildinfo write.
Recommendations:
1. Add local content health and degraded-mode states - Create a content registry that validates local datasets at startup and renders explicit healthy, empty, malformed, and degraded states.
2. Normalize compensation analytics - Replace string-only compensation bands with typed records for role, firm, location, year, base, signing, bonus, hourly/intern pay, source, and caveats; add comparison UI and tests.
3. Build Resources and ML prep sections - Add structured local resources plus an ML preparation page covering supervised/unsupervised learning, regression, classification, bias-variance, validation, and finance examples.
4. Add Options Intuition mini-game - Implement local original prompts for delta, gamma, theta, volatility, moneyness, payoff diagrams, and directional intuition with validation and smoke coverage.
5. Document source-aware c [truncated from 1787 characters]

agents.name: Coding Pass 1
agents.taskPrompt:
Make the largest coherent, reviewable change that materially advances the scoped outcome, preserve existing conventions, prioritize correctness, run relevant checks, and explain changed files and verification results.
Scoped goal from the goal agent: Build one coherent question-bank extension: local answer frameworks plus a deterministic mock-interview pract…
Implement a source-aware mock interview and answer-framework layer for the existing local Question Bank. Focus on src/App.tsx, src/lib/questionBankSearch.ts, src/lib/masteryStorage.ts, smoke/question-bank.spec.ts, and add narrowly scoped data/lib/test files if needed. Add typed local answer frameworks for the major question-bank topics, map questions to the right framework, and expose a #/mock or Question Bank mock-practice route that builds deterministic sessions from local filters such as firm, role, topic, difficulty, and source category. Each session should show timeboxes, selected questions, matched framework steps, common mistakes/sanity checks, source notes, uncertainty, and existing mastery controls for question-bank items. Avoid reworking already-evidenced mental math, roadmap, company page, or original-practice-question batches unless a test breaks. Do not add live APIs, accounts, scraping, paid services, copied prep-book content, or broad refactors. Done means the app has a reviewable local mock-interview mode with direct unit/smoke/build evidence and no regression to existing routes. Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.
Keep the work scoped to one coherent coding pass. Prefer the largest reviewable batch of related implementation, tests, and evidence that fits the approved recommendation. If the recommendation still feels broad, narrow it to the strongest cohesive batch rather than a tiny evidence-only slice. Do not start unrelated follow-up work.
Acceptance criteria:
- A typed local answer-framework model covers probability, expected value, brain teasers, market making/trading, coding/data, and ML/statistics with steps, sanity checks, common mistakes, and provenance/uncertainty text.
- A deterministic mock-session builder creates bounded sessions from local questionBank filters, maps every selected question to a framework, and handles sparse filters with a clear empty or fallback state.
- The UI exposes the mock-interview flow from the dashboard or Question Bank and renders session timeboxes, framework guidance, source notes, uncertainty, review-detail links, and mastery controls.
- Question detail or cards show the matched answer framework so practice is guided by reusable reasoning patterns, not just final explanations.
- Existing company pages, mini-games, roadmap, question-bank search, and mastery persistence continue to pass their current tests.
- No runtime dependency on external APIs, credentials, scraping, user accounts, or network-only data is introduced.
Integrity will verify with:
- Run targeted unit tests for the new framework/session builder plus existing questionBankSearch and masteryStorage coverage.
- Update/run smoke/question-bank.spec.ts to verify Question Bank to mock flow, framework rendering, source/uncertainty text, and mastery controls.
- Run npm run test for deterministic local logic regressions.
- Run npm run build to confirm TypeScript and Vite production build pass.
Ultimate Goal: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.

- Added small helpers/tests in `questionBankSearch` and `masteryStorage`.
- Updated `smoke/question-bank.spec.ts` for the Question Bank → mock flow.

agents.name: Goal Agent
agents.taskPrompt:
Turn approved recommendations into scoped, testable execution briefs. Keep plans concrete, bounded, aligned with the outcome strategy, and focused on evidence that moves the Ultimate Goal checklist forward.
Turn the approved recommendation into a scoped goal for the next coding pass.
The output must represent one bounded task for a single cycle, not a broad multi-phase project.
The result must be executable by one coding agent in one pass. If related checks share implementation paths, tests, or evidence, plan the largest coherent reviewable batch instead of narrowing to a tiny evidence-only slice.
Do not scope another coding pass just to re-prove a semantically identical checklist batch that already has direct evidence. If the approved recommendation is about checklist cleanup, focus the brief on the concrete missing evidence or redundant checks named in the recommendation.
`executionBrief` should be a full prompt for the coding agent: what to change, where to focus, what to avoid, and how to know the task is done.
Make acceptanceCriteria concrete and testable.
Keep acceptanceCriteria to at most 6 bullets and testStrategy to at most 4 focused checks.
Use the Goal checklist as the completion source of truth. Prefer a scoped plan that turns a coherent group of unmet or unknown required checks into met checks with evidence.
Keep constraints aligned with the Ultimate Goal and the repository boundaries.
Use the outcome strategy below to keep the scoped plan pointed at the best finished project outcome.
Outcome strategy:
- Ultimate target: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
- Completion source of truth: 50/122 required goal checks met (41%).
- Primary next move: Satisfy the highest-impact coherent batch of related goal checks with direct repository evidence.
- Highest-impact open checks: Each company page includes a “How to Prepare” section with recommended platform drills, topics to review, common mistakes, and a suggested 1-week and…; Each company page includes a company overview, business model summary, role types, office locations where relevant, recruiting timeline, interview pr…; Each company page includes a compensation section for internship and full-time roles, with approximate ranges, year/date context, location notes, sou…; Each company page includes a dedicated question section showing firm-associated questions, reported question categories, similar original practice qu….
- Efficient batching opportunity: includes: The app includes a mental math speed game with arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, expected value arithm…; The app includes a recruiting roadmap that organizes preparation by week, topic, difficulty, and target firm, with recommended sequences for someone…; The app includes individual company pages for major quant firms including Jane Street, Citadel Securities, Citadel, Two Sigma, D. E. Shaw, Optiver, S…; The app includes original practice questions created specifically for the platform, with realistic quant-interview style wording and detailed explana….
- Current blockers: none.
- Quality bar: The final project should feel like a polished, professional quant recruiting preparation terminal: fast, structured, rigorous, source-aware, visually appealing, and genuinely useful for daily practice. Core functionalit…
- Preserve constraints: If an existing project stack is already present, preserve the existing stack, conventions, scripts, routing, styling system, and architecture unless…; If this is a greenfield project, prefer a local-first React, TypeScript, and Vite-style web app with structured local data files and local persistenc…; The website must be able t [truncated from 16026 characters]
agents.lastMessageSnippet: Summary: Build one coherent question-bank extension: local answer frameworks plus a deterministic mock-interview practice route, using existing evidenced company, roadmap, mental-math, and question-bank data as the substrate.

commandLog.command: /bin/bash -lc "grep -n \" function getAllLocalPracticeItems\\|getAllLocalPracticeItems\" -n src/App.tsx && sed -n '280,360p' src/App.tsx && sed -n '1,260p' smoke/company-pages.spec.ts && sed -n '1,260p' smoke/mini-games.spec.ts"

/bin/bash -lc "grep -n \" Question Bank\\|questions\" -n src/App.tsx | head -80 && sed -n '1,260p' src/lib/masteryStorage.ts && printf '\\n--- mastery tests ---\\n' && sed -n '1,260p' src/lib/masteryStorage.test.ts && printf '\\n--- dashboard ---\\n' && sed -n '1,260p' src/lib/masteryDashboard.ts"

/bin/bash -lc "sed -n '1,260p' src/data/questionBank.ts && printf '\\n--- validation ---\\n' && sed -n '1,300p' src/lib/questionBankValidation.ts && printf '\\n--- validation tests ---\\n' && sed -n '1,260p' src/lib/questionBankValidation.test.ts"

/bin/bash -lc "sed -n '1,260p' src/lib/questionBankTypes.ts && printf '\\n--- search ---\\n' && sed -n '1,260p' src/lib/questionBankSearch.ts && printf '\\n--- tests ---\\n' && sed -n '1,260p' src/lib/questionBankSearch.test.ts && printf '\\n--- smoke qb ---\\n' && sed -n '1,240p' smoke/question-bank.spec.ts"
/bin/bash -lc "find src smoke -maxdepth 3 -type f | sort && printf '\\n--- package ---\\n' && sed -n '1,220p' package.json"

events.title: Agent message
events.detail: Summary: Build one coherent question-bank extension: local answer frameworks plus a deterministic mock-interview practice route, using existing evidenced company, roadmap, mental-math, and question-bank data as the substrate.
events.raw: {"type":"agentMessage","id":"msg_0b2885cb64872004016a0355bb56a4819181aa153444123d15","text":"{\"summary\":\"Build one coherent question-bank extension: local answer frameworks plus a deterministic mock-interview practice route, using existing evidenced company, roadmap, mental-math, and question-bank data as the substrate.\",\"executionBrief\":\"Implement a source-aware mock interview and answer-framework layer for the existing local Question Bank. Focus on src/App.tsx, src/lib/questionBankSearch.ts, src/lib/masteryStorage.ts, smoke/question-bank.spec.ts, and add narrowly scoped data/lib/test files if needed. Add typed local answer frameworks for the major question-bank topics, map questions to the right framework, and expose a #/mock or Question Bank mock-practice route that builds deterministic sessions from local filters such as firm, role, topic, difficulty, and source category. Each session should show timeboxes, selected questions, matched framework steps, common mistakes/sanity checks, source notes, uncertainty, and existing mastery controls for question-bank items. Avoid reworking already-evidenced mental math, roadmap, company page, or original-practice-question batches unless a test breaks. Do not add live APIs, accounts, scraping, paid services, copied prep-book content, or broad refactors. Done means the app has a reviewable local mock-interview mode with direct unit/smoke/build evidence and no regression to existing routes.\",\"ac [truncated from 2178 characters]
